task: I’m putting together a simple end-of-unit checklist for a hands-on class project and want it to be solid before I use it. Can you help me make a practical review sheet that catches mistakes, makes it clear when something is actually finished, and keeps safety from getting overlooked?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a practical end-of-unit review sheet you can use for a hands-on class project. I’ve kept it classroom-ready, clear, and focused on finish quality, common mistakes, and safety.

## End-of-Unit Project Review Sheet

**Student name:** ____________________  
**Project:** ____________________  
**Date:** ____________________  
**Instructor:** ____________________

### 1) Project is complete
Check each box before turning in.

- [ ] All required parts are included
- [ ] All steps in the project plan were completed
- [ ] Measurements match the project requirements
- [ ] Fasteners/joints are secure
- [ ] Project functions as intended
- [ ] No missing, loose, or damaged parts

### 2) Quality check
Look over the project carefully.

- [ ] Edges are smooth / no sharp spots
- [ ] Surfaces are clean and finished
- [ ] Parts are aligned correctly
- [ ] Work is neat and professional
- [ ] No obvious damage from tools or handling
- [ ] Any labels, marks, or markings are correct

### 3) Common mistakes to catch
Check for these before calling it done.

- [ ] Wrong measurement or cut
- [ ] Loose hardware or weak connection
- [ ] Uneven assembly or poor alignment
- [ ] Tool marks left unfinished
- [ ] Missed step in the instructions
- [ ] Dirt, debris, or glue/paint where it should not be

### 4) Safety check
Safety still matters at the end.

- [ ] Tools are turned off and put away properly
- [ ] Work area is clear of scrap and debris
- [ ] PPE is removed only after tools are safe
- [ ] No exposed sharp edges, hot surfaces, or pinch points
- [ ] Electrical cords / batteries / power sources are secured
- [ ] Project can be handled safely by the next person

### 5) Student self-review
Answer before submitting.

- What part of the project turned out best?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What problem did you fix or work through?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What would you improve if you had one more class period?  
  ______________________________________________________

### 6) Final check-off
Instructor or student lead initials each item.

- [ ] Project is finished to standard
- [ ] Safety checks passed
- [ ] Quality checks passed
- [ ] Student can explain what was done and why
- [ ] Project is ready to submit / display / store

**Initials:** ________

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **one-page printable version**
- a **teacher check-off form**
- or a **version for a specific project** like woodworking, welding, automotive, or basic shop tools.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good start. It’s usable, but it’s still a little generic for a shop class. I’d want it tightened up so a student can’t just eyeball it and say “looks fine.”

Add a few harder checks:
- measurements within tolerance
- all welds / joints inspected, not just “secure”
- machine settings returned to safe position
- guards in place
- no borrowed tools missing
- cleanup done to shop standard, not just “clear”

Also, I’d separate **student self-check** from **instructor sign-off**. Those are not the same thing. Students will mark things done that are not done if you let them.

If you want it solid, I’d make it into a simple three-part sheet:
1. **Completion**
2. **Safety**
3. **Quality / tolerance**

Then a final line: **“Not finished until instructor verifies.”** That keeps the standard clear.

If you want, I can help you tighten this into a cleaner one-page version with a few trade-school style checkboxes that are harder to game.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter shop-style version that’s harder to game and clearer about what “done” actually means.

---

# End-of-Unit Project Review Sheet  
**Student:** ____________________  
**Project:** ____________________  
**Date:** ____________________  
**Instructor:** ____________________

## 1) Completion
Check each item before the project can be considered finished.

- [ ] All required parts are present
- [ ] All required steps were completed
- [ ] Measurements are within tolerance: ____________________
- [ ] All joints / welds / fasteners are complete
- [ ] No missing parts, loose parts, or unfinished sections
- [ ] Project functions as intended

## 2) Safety
Do not skip this section.

- [ ] Guards are in place
- [ ] Machine settings returned to safe position
- [ ] Power is off / disconnected before inspection or cleanup
- [ ] PPE is removed only after the work is safe
- [ ] No sharp edges, hot surfaces, pinch points, or exposed hazards
- [ ] Work area is clear of scrap, debris, and spills
- [ ] Borrowed tools are accounted for and returned

## 3) Quality / Tolerance
This is where “looks fine” is not enough.

- [ ] Measurements checked against print / instructions
- [ ] Parts are aligned correctly
- [ ] Joints / welds inspected for strength and finish
- [ ] Fasteners are tight and properly installed
- [ ] Surface finish meets class standard
- [ ] No obvious tool damage, burn marks, tear-out, or sloppy cleanup
- [ ] Work meets the required tolerance: ____________________

## 4) Student Self-Check
Student completes this before sign-off.

- What did you check before calling it done?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What problem did you find and correct?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What still needs instructor review?  
  ______________________________________________________

## 5) Instructor Sign-Off
**Important:** Not finished until instructor verifies.

- [ ] Completion verified
- [ ] Safety verified
- [ ] Quality / tolerance verified
- [ ] Student cleanup meets shop standard
- [ ] Project approved as finished

**Instructor initials:** ___________

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean one-page printable form** with tighter wording and more checkbox style, so it’s ready to hand out.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. But I’d tighten two things before I’d hand it out.

First, the tolerance line needs to be less mushy. If you leave it as a blank every time, some kids will write something vague like “close enough.” I’d want the sheet to force the actual tolerance or reference the print/spec right there.

Second, I’d make the instructor sign-off heavier. Not just a general approval — more like the instructor confirms completion, safety, and quality separately. That keeps the student from treating self-check as the finish line.

I’d also add one line for machine condition if this is a shop class:
- [ ] Machine and tools left in proper condition for the next user

That catches the habit of leaving settings, vises, clamps, or debris for somebody else to deal with.

If you want, I’d take this one more step and make it look like something a vocational program would actually use — plain, strict, no filler.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a stricter, shop-style version with the tolerance line tied to the print/spec and a heavier instructor sign-off.

---

# End-of-Unit Project Review Sheet

**Student:** ____________________  
**Project:** ____________________  
**Date:** ____________________  
**Instructor:** ____________________

## 1) Completion
Check each item before the project can be considered finished.

- [ ] All required parts are present
- [ ] All required steps were completed
- [ ] Measurements match the print/spec: ____________________
- [ ] All joints / welds / fasteners are complete
- [ ] No missing parts, loose parts, or unfinished sections
- [ ] Project functions as intended

## 2) Safety
Do not skip this section.

- [ ] Guards are in place
- [ ] Machine settings returned to safe position
- [ ] Power is off / disconnected before inspection or cleanup
- [ ] PPE is removed only after the work is safe
- [ ] No sharp edges, hot surfaces, pinch points, or exposed hazards
- [ ] Work area is clear of scrap, debris, and spills
- [ ] Borrowed tools are accounted for and returned

## 3) Quality / Tolerance
This is where “looks fine” is not enough.

- [ ] Measurements checked against print / instructions
- [ ] Parts are aligned correctly
- [ ] Joints / welds inspected for strength and finish
- [ ] Fasteners are tight and properly installed
- [ ] Surface finish meets class standard
- [ ] No obvious tool damage, burn marks, tear-out, or sloppy cleanup
- [ ] Work meets required tolerance: ____________________

## 4) Machine / Tool Condition
Leave the shop ready for the next person.

- [ ] Machine and tools left in proper condition for the next user
- [ ] Settings returned to normal / safe position
- [ ] Vises, clamps, and accessories reset or stored properly
- [ ] Tool count is complete
- [ ] No borrowed tools missing

## 5) Student Self-Check
Student completes this before sign-off.

- What did you check before calling it done?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What problem did you find and correct?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What still needs instructor review?  
  ______________________________________________________

## 6) Instructor Sign-Off
**Not finished until instructor verifies each section.**

- [ ] Completion verified
- [ ] Safety verified
- [ ] Quality / tolerance verified
- [ ] Machine / tool condition verified
- [ ] Cleanup meets shop standard
- [ ] Project approved as finished

**Instructor initials:** ___________

---

If you want, I can make this into a **clean one-page classroom handout** with tighter wording and a more trade-school look.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. But I’d still tighten the wording so it reads like a shop form, not a nice draft.

Two things:
- That tolerance line still needs to force something specific. Either the print/spec reference or the actual tolerance range. Blank line by itself is too easy to game.
- The instructor sign-off should be broken out by section, not one general approval at the end. Completion, safety, quality, machine/tool condition. Separate checks. That matters.

Also, I’d drop a couple of the softer phrases. Stuff like “meets class standard” is fine, but if you want students to take it seriously, make the language plain and hard to wiggle around.

If you want, I’d take this version and turn it into a true one-page handout with a stricter layout and cleaner shop wording.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, shop-style one-page version with harder wording and separate instructor checks.

---

# End-of-Unit Project Review Sheet

**Student:** ____________________  
**Project:** ____________________  
**Date:** ____________________  
**Instructor:** ____________________

## 1) Completion
Check each item before the project can be called finished.

- [ ] All required parts are present
- [ ] All required steps were completed
- [ ] Measurements match print/spec: ____________________
- [ ] Tolerance required: ____________________  
  **or**  
  [ ] Tolerance range on print/spec: ____________________
- [ ] All joints / welds / fasteners are complete
- [ ] No missing parts, loose parts, or unfinished sections
- [ ] Project works as intended

## 2) Safety
Do not skip this section.

- [ ] Guards are in place
- [ ] Machine settings returned to safe position
- [ ] Power is off / disconnected before inspection or cleanup
- [ ] PPE is removed only after the work is safe
- [ ] No sharp edges, hot surfaces, pinch points, or exposed hazards
- [ ] Work area is clear of scrap, debris, and spills
- [ ] Borrowed tools are accounted for and returned

## 3) Quality / Tolerance
“Looks fine” is not enough.

- [ ] Measurements checked against print / instructions
- [ ] Parts are aligned correctly
- [ ] Joints / welds inspected for strength and finish
- [ ] Fasteners are tight and properly installed
- [ ] Surface finish is complete and clean
- [ ] No burn marks, tear-out, tool damage, or sloppy cleanup
- [ ] Work is within required tolerance: ____________________

## 4) Machine / Tool Condition
Leave the shop ready for the next user.

- [ ] Machine and tools left in proper condition
- [ ] Settings returned to normal / safe position
- [ ] Vises, clamps, and accessories reset or stored properly
- [ ] Tool count is complete
- [ ] No borrowed tools missing

## 5) Student Self-Check
Student fills this out before instructor review.

- What did you check before calling it done?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What problem did you find and correct?  
  ______________________________________________________

- What still needs instructor review?  
  ______________________________________________________

## 6) Instructor Sign-Off
**Not finished until instructor verifies each section.**

### Completion
- [ ] Verified

### Safety
- [ ] Verified

### Quality / Tolerance
- [ ] Verified

### Machine / Tool Condition
- [ ] Verified

### Cleanup
- [ ] Meets shop standard

**Instructor initials:** ___________  
**Project approved as finished:** [ ] Yes  [ ] No

---

If you want, I can also turn this into a **cleaner classroom form** with tighter spacing and fewer words, so it looks more like a real shop handout.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the structure I wanted. The separate instructor checks and the tolerance line tied to the print/spec make it a lot harder to game. That reads more like an actual shop form now, not a worksheet.

I’d still make one tiny tweak before handing it out: change “or” on the tolerance line so it’s only one required field, not a choice. Either the print/spec calls it out, or the instructor writes the tolerance range in there. Don’t leave both open.

But overall, this is solid enough to use.
